data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: Forward Estimated Growth Is Really Remarkable, Revenue Growth Too
data.description:
Originally published on April 19, 2026

When the news gets too good in the stock market, it can be as much of a warning sign as an incentive for either equity allocations to float higher in client accounts to take advantage of bullish economic and corporate conditions or trim positions and raise a little cash.

Here’s a good post from Ryan Detrick of Carson Group that shows the S&P 500’s 7% jump in 7 days and then “expected forward returns”.

The S&P 500 is back to overbought already, but fundamentals remain pretty solid - as the rest of this blog details (supporting the technical analysis).

Each week, this blog updates S&P 500 forward EPS estimates using LSEG data, which allows me to look at revisions to forward estimates and see which sectors might be a safe place to allocate client funds or trim some gains from a particular sector.

Usually at this time of the quarter, with Q1 ’26 earnings just starting, S&P 500 EPS watchers are seeing slightly negative or downward revisions to Q2, Q3, and Q4 numbers, as the tendency is to be cautious about the forward quarter’s as current results - in this case Q1 ’26 - reflect that conservatism and show an “upside surprise” to estimates. Particularly with Iran and its headlines, I thought some weakness would occur in the forward estimates, and so far that’s not been the case.

Q1 and Q2 ’26 expected S&P 500 EPS growth is still relatively flat with a slight positive bias, but look at Q3 ’26 expected EPS growth revision to +22.4% today, versus the +14.4% on January 2nd, ’26. Tech peaked in late October ’25, but I can’t recall any particular damage to any sector that would have caused downward revisions to any particular sector or sectors in Q3 ’25 that would have resulted in unusually easy compares for the S&P 500.

So, what is the market discounting in Q3 ’26, or are these just “bad numbers” from the Street?

To try and get some educated estimate of what’s happening, let’s look at expected S&P 500 revenue growth (in the above table, look at the last four columns, which show expected S&P 500 revenue growth by quarter in 2026):

Note the 200 bp acceleration for Q1 and Q2 ’26, which is a pretty healthy revision, since revenue “upside surprise” factors are typically 1-2.0%. Note also that expected revenue growth for the S&P 500 in each quarter in 2026 is higher than the actual average revenue growth per quarter in 2025.

2025 actual revenue growth by the S&P 500 “averaged” 7.2%. Here’s the progression by quarter in 2025 in terms of actual revenue growth:

  • Q4 ’25: +9.2%
  • Q3 ’25: +8.3%
  • Q2 ’25: +6.4%
  • Q1 ’25: +5.0%

What might be even more interesting is that looking at actual revenue growth for the S&P 500 by quarter since Q4 ’12 (the first quarter this blog began tracking quarterly “actual” S&P 500 EPS and revenue data), S&P 500 revenue growth “averaged” +3.2% from Q4 ’12 to Q4 ’19. The strongest revenue quarters within that time period happened with the “Tax Cuts And Jobs Act” passed in Q4 ’17. Here’s how those quarters tracked in terms of revenue growth:

  • Q3 ’18: +8.6%
  • Q2 ’18: +8.5%
  • Q1 ’18: +8.4%
  • Q4 ’17: +8.3%

Because these revenue growth rates are “post TCJA”, and then after 2018 the revenue growth rate slows again into 2019 (and then we have the Covid mess), it’s easier to conclude that what we are seeing today in the S&P 500 is probably considerably aided by the “One Big Beautiful Bill” (OBBB) even in the face of tariffs of 2025, and now the crude oil shock of 2026.

The tech sector is expecting 27.1% revenue growth in Q1 ’26, along with expected 46% EPS growth.

To answer the above question about Q3 ’26 and potential bad numbers, Q3 ’26 might be lapping peak OBBB or the 2025 tariffs, it might be an implied forecast for crude oil and having it come down to the pre-Iran level of $67 per barrel (WTI).

S&P 500 data

  • The forward 4-quarter estimate (FFQE) for the S&P 500 rose this past week to $340.96 from $339.22 last week.
  • The S&P 500 P/E on the forward estimate rose to 20.9x this week.
  • The S&P 500 “earnings yield” (SP EY) fell to 4.78% this past week on the sharp bounce in the S&P 500. The +5% EY yield in late March ’26 was a good tell for stocks, but I prefer not to make predictions around metrics such as these.
  • While just 48 companies have reported their Q1 ’26 financial results so far, the EPS upside surprise is still quite healthy at 8.8%, while the S&P 500 revenue upside surprise is also healthy at +2.2%. Another 94 companies are expected to report this week, per the LSEG report “This Week in Earnings”.

Summary

Having tracked a lot of this data (from LSEG) from post-2008 until today, the remarkable aspect of this current period is the lack of any negativity or discouragement from analysts in terms of seeing quarterly forward numbers taken down, as is usually the pattern.

Financials had a very good quarter last week. Some Citi (C) was added after their earnings release, while this blog owns enough of JPM already.

This week, earnings previews will be written on IBM and BA, two turnaround stocks. BA is struggling as they near peak revenue in 2018, and yet, their margins and operating income and EPS are nowhere near records, which tells me that the FAA restriction on deliveries every quarter is keeping BA from leveraging the manufacturing base - but that what’s got them into trouble to begin with.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All EPS and revenue data is sourced from LSEG. Readers and clients should monitor their own comfort with portfolio volatility and adjust accordingly.

Original Post

data.title: Weekly Market Pulse: Stop Reading The News
data.description:
Stock markets the world over celebrated the end of the Iran War last week, with many closing the week at all-time highs: large cap (S&P 500, Russell 1000), small cap (Russell 2000, S&P 600), NASDAQ, emerging markets, Asia ex-Japan, Latin America 40, Canada, Australia, Israel, Poland, Italy, Netherlands, Austria, Spain, Singapore, Taiwan, and Brazil all closed at all-time highs (based on their ETFs). The S&P 500 closed higher every day last week and is up 12 of the last 13 days. Crude oil, meanwhile, fell over 11% last week, closing at $85.57, down from $117 just 8 trading days ago. It is still up 27% from the pre-war close but down 27% from the recent high. Happy days are here again?

Well, maybe, maybe not. President Trump assured markets last week that the Strait of Hormuz was open for all commercial ship traffic and Iran had “agreed to everything”, including giving up its stockpile of uranium. He went so far as to claim that there was a plan to send “our people” into Iran with “lots of excavators” to retrieve the uranium and bring it back to the US. Iran had also agreed not to pursue nuclear weapons for a period beyond 20 years and to stop backing all their proxy groups like Hamas and Hezbollah. There were rumors of $20 billion in cash changing hands, but that was denied by the President.

Those claims stood all the way to Saturday morning when Iran announced that the Strait of Hormuz was not, in fact, open and that transit required permission from their military. Ships in the gulf are getting really good at U-turns. They also denied agreeing to give up their uranium and said that shipping it to the US was a “non-starter”. While they didn’t directly deny the claim about Hamas and Hezbollah, neither did they confirm it. The Iranians referred to President Trump’s statements as “big lies” and “maximalist demands”.

So, did President Trump lie? Well, that may depend on the definition of “lie”. We don’t know what the President was trying to accomplish with his statements, and it could be part of some negotiating strategy, but if so, I’ll be damned if I can figure out what it is. The Strait of Hormuz has allegedly been open for 10 days now, but the number of ships going through it can probably be counted on two hands (okay, you might need a few toes). The simple answer is that regardless of what either side has said or done, the strait is still closed, and the oil, fertilizer, and other commodities stuck in the gulf are still stuck.

None of this, by the way, is exactly a secret. There are some ships getting through sporadically, but the traffic is easily tracked (see here and here) and it’s a fraction of what it was pre-war. And the idea that the Iranians would give up their uranium after resisting all this time in exchange for, well, nothing, is fanciful. Stop backing Hamas and Hezbollah? When they’ve conditioned opening the strait on Israel stopping their attacks on the same in Lebanon? Again, not exactly rocket science to figure out that was unlikely. So my question then is, why did markets move as they did last week? Why did oil drop almost 10% Friday? Why did stocks make new all-time highs?

Markets are said to anticipate, but that is only true when they’re right. Markets are not precognizant. They don’t actually divine the future but instead reflect the consensus opinion – which may or may not be right. These markets are, after all, a reflection of the people who trade them, those very emotional, fallible humans. Is that still true? Are markets really a reflection of the decisions made by millions of individuals? Well, that is an interesting question, isn’t it? What role does AI play in markets today? And if AI is being used to trade, do all AI systems come to the same conclusions? Or are they different enough that two AI programs could observe the same press releases, off-hand Presidential comments, market movements, and other inputs and come to different conclusions about the future direction of the global economy and markets? Here’s another question: if AI is prone to hallucinations – and I can attest first hand that it is – then what role would those hallucinations play in markets? All good questions with not very good answers right now.

Is the stock market responding to Iran War developments or something else? It seems obvious that the war is impacting stocks, since they rally when there is "good" news about the war and fall when there is "bad" news, but a lot of the initial moves get reversed pretty quickly. In the short term, markets are moved by the emotional response to “news,” but over the longer term, stock prices are a function of earnings growth, dividends, and interest rates. The Iran War should only affect stocks to the degree it affects those variables. It would seem, given movements in interest rates and stock prices, that the impact of the war has been pretty minimal to date. Interest rates (10-year Treasury rate) are up 28 basis points since the start of the war and are trading in line with where they were at the beginning of February. That just isn’t enough to affect stock prices much.

Q1 earnings have been good so far, but only 10% of companies have reported. Those 10% have been good though, with 88% of them reporting earnings better than expected. A surprising addendum is that those companies have, on average, seen their stock prices fall over the period two days before to two days after they report. That is highly unusual; most companies that report better-than-expected earnings see their stock prices rise. What does it mean? Well, it’s too early to make a general market observation, but for those companies at least, I’d say that good, even great, earnings were already priced in. A stock that doesn’t rise on good news is one where supply has met demand.

AI certainly gets some credit for where the market is today, but I don’t think that tells the whole story. There is little doubt that AI hype is getting out of hand, and I would point to the story of Allbirds (BIRD) last week as definitive evidence. This former sneaker company, that once sported a market cap of $4 billion, sold its sneaker brand for $39 million – yes, that “m” is right – in March. Last week it announced that it was raising $50 million through a private placement of debt to fund their transition to “…(A) fully integrated GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS) and AI-native cloud solutions provider.” The stock was up over 700% at one point the day of the announcement, but in what passes for sanity these days, ended the week up a mere 350%. So, they’re going to compete in the “GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS) and AI-native cloud solutions provider” market on $50 million? A company that was formed as a public benefit corporation, dedicated to environmental conservation, and made sneakers out of wool and eucalyptus fiber is dropping the public benefit moniker so they can pursue a business that is the antithesis of their original mission? I’m guessing the smell of patchouli has dissipated in the NewBird AI office. But microcap stocks aren’t moving the S&P 500.

The Magnificent 7 stocks have performed well in this recovery too, but if we’re talking about fundamentals, then the market’s resilience isn’t really about them either. As I said above, Q1 earnings for S&P 500 companies so far are coming in better than expected (up about 13%). And full-year earnings are expected to rise 18%, about as good as you’re going to see outside of a recession recovery. But the Magnificent 7 isn’t so magnificent when it comes to earnings expectations. The Mag 7 have been growing faster than the other 493 companies in the index*, and that is expected to continue in this quarter and for the full year. But that is due to one company – Nvidia (NVDA). Earnings for the not-quite-so-magnificent 6 are expected to rise only 6.4% in Q1, while the other 493 companies’ earnings are expected to rise 10.1%. For CY 2026, the mediocre 6 are expected to report earnings gains of 13.2%, while the forgotten 493 are expected to report a rise of 15.9%. Furthermore, S&P 400 (midcap) and S&P 600 (small cap) earnings are expected to rise 14.5% and 15.5%, respectively, in Q1 and 17% and 19%, respectively, for CY 2026. Stock market performance is no longer just about a few big companies.

If you were only paying attention to the things that matter for stock prices – earnings growth, dividend yields, and interest rates – the bullish stance of the market makes perfect sense. It is only when you start reading the news that the bears are able to get their claws in you. John Bogle’s simple model for the stock market was that returns would approximate earnings growth + dividend yield + valuation change (driven primarily by interest rates). Doing that today gets you 18% earnings growth + 1.4% dividend yield (and said dividends are expected to grow 6.5% this year) + valuation change. If interest rates are fairly stable – and they have been for the last 3 years – and valuations don’t change, we’re looking at nearly 20% gains.

There are only so many things we can do to improve our investment returns. Keep costs under control and limit trading are two well-known ways of doing so. Another one that is related to the mandate to limit trading is to limit how often you check the balance of your account. That comes from a study by Shlomo Benartzi and Richard Thaler called "Myopic Loss Aversion and The Equity Premium Puzzle".

The idea is simple. On any given day, the odds of the stock market being up or down are approximately 50% (the actual figure is 53% up), so if you look at your account every day, you’re going to see a loss about half the time. If you look once a year, the odds you’ll see a gain improve to about 75%, and if you check once a decade, you’ll see a gain about 95% of the time. Since investors react more strongly to losses than gains, checking less frequently lowers the chance that you’ll panic-sell. There is another “study” that may be apocryphal, by Fidelity that found that the best-performing accounts at the firm were those that were frozen due to probate and couldn’t trade or were held by investors who lost their log-in credentials or just stopped checking their accounts for some other reason.

So, to our list of things that are likely to improve your results, we can add, stop checking your account balance. And for goodness' sake, stop reading the news**.

Joseph Calhoun

*I know some smarty pants is going to tell me that there are 503 stocks in the S&P 500. They are right, but there are only 500 companies in the index. Google, Fox, and News Corp. each have 2 classes of stock in the index for a total of 503 stocks

**I don’t mean this literally. There are good reasons to be informed about the world. But with all the sources of mis- and disinformation these days, you need to be very careful about translating “news” into portfolio actions.

data.title: MPLX: Boasting Strong ROIC As Distribution Growth Could Accelerate
data.description:
Investment Thesis - Updates Since Last Coverage

This isn't my first time writing about one of the best-in-class MLPs in the market right now. That sentence alone should set the tone for this article I think. MPLX LP (MPLX) is currently sitting at a 7.71% yield with strong growth potential here. Since my time on Seeking Alpha I’ve spent a lot of time writing about midstream companies with strong dividend/distribution policies. MPLX ticks this box along with being well positioned as a provider for pipeline capacity in the Permian Basin following the rapid rise in “associated gas” now being produced after record years of oil drilling.

But even if MPLX looks like a solid choice right now, and an obvious one, the return since my last coverage back in December 2025 has meant an underperformance of the S&P 500. Now, most people who actually invest in MPLX have a timeline longer than 4 months. I know I certainly do. The actual long-term returns rely on MPLX will be able to provide a 7-8% yield consistently along with unit price appreciation coming from MPLX doing more acquisitions, more pipeline expansion. Both of these seem true as MPLX is guiding for $2.4 billion in growth capital alone this year. I was bullish on the stock last year and I am still very much so. Rating MPLX a Strong Buy.

Unit Tax Implications

With MLPs there are additional or other tax implications to be aware of. I’ve covered this in detail in my last article, so I’ll just input a quick paragraph to provide some info for anyone not aware of how this works.

The concept is that these partnerships often have large asset bases which means high amounts of non-cash depreciation expenses. These deductions would often mean the taxable income for the partnership is lower than the actual distributions. The cash that is then distributed that exceeds the taxable income would be considered a return of capital and helps to lower the investor's average cost per unit.

The main difference being that you’d receive a Schedule K-1 instead of a Form 1099-DIV. This then also arrives later and often involves more personal work. For long-term investors this isn’t really an issue, I think, the upside and returns usually outweigh the extra few steps by a lot.

Operational Overview

There are 2 main parts of MPLX: the logistics and storage part, followed by gathering and processing. The actual reporting is done in 2 segments as well, roughly divided into crude oil and natural gas operations. The latter is where MPLX has seen the most amount of adj. EBITDA growth over the past few years. In 2023 the segment reported $5.233 billion in adj EBITDA, which has since grown to $6.423 billion. A 7.56% annual growth pace is certainly acceptable for any stable midstream company. In the crude oil segment, the figure grew 2.9% per year instead.

Combined, MPLX generated $7.017 billion in adjusted EBITDA last year, marking a 3.96% growth rate over 3 years. Given the demand for increased pipeline capacity in the Permian Basin and MPLX’s strong position to supply this I would estimate that it will also be able to command more premium pricing for this service. This should directly hit the bottom line and we’ll see the adj EBITDA rise at a faster pace than it has the past 3 years.

I know I’ve mentioned the yield at the start of the article quite a lot, but it really is one of the main attractions with MPLX right now. In 2025, distributions totaled $4.024 billion. Looking back to the earlier picture, it generated $5.791 billion in DCF, meaning that it only has a payout ratio of 69.4% TTM. This is well within reason and means that investors have a moderate amount of downside protection right here as well, meaning it would take a lot for MPLX to not be able to distribute the same amount of capital at the very least.

Why MPLX is able to do this really comes down to how its operations are structured. Midstream companies are notorious for producing incredibly stable returns over a longer period. It’s a never-ending debate whether or not these companies are immune to commodity fluctuation. I think they are. Just look at how the price of oil has moved the past 5 years. Then look at how little that affected the crude oil segment, nothing, it kept on rising.

Again, this comes back to the nature of the operations. At first MPLX was the creation of Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC) back in 2012. The purpose was to own, operate and build out the midstream infrastructure.

We continue to have a strategic relationship with MPC, which is a large source of our revenues. We have executed numerous long-term, fee-based agreements with minimum volume commitments with MPC which provide us with a stable and predictable revenue stream and source of cash flows.

That MPC is still a majority owner, owning 64% of the shares does perhaps mean that negotiating the rates will be more favorable to MPC rather than MPLX. Still, as I’ve echoed in this article already, it’s the predictability I am after, and this is what enables MPLX to raise the distributions on a consistent basis year after year.

A Moat On Sour Gas Following Massive Acquisition

As I mentioned earlier, MPLX is raising its growth capex heavily this year and it’s very concentrated in the natural gas segment. The 150% increase is worth talking about.

90% of our growth capital will be directed towards our natural gas and NGL services segment, where we see some of the most compelling opportunities in the midstream sector.

Management is clearly pivoting to the many natural gas opportunities that are opening up now, be that the associated gas I mentioned earlier or the massive electricity demand coming from data centers where natural gas seems to be the most reliable and scalable option at the moment.

The projects that MPLX is investing in right now exist in the Permian and Marcellus basins. Both of which are expected to generate mid-teen returns once they are operational, which according to the last earnings call is in 2028 and the years after.

Right now the Permian is shifting away from the “sweeter” crude it has extracted for years and even decades and into the deeper formations like Avalon and Bone Spring which are inside the Delaware Basin. The issue here for the drilling companies is that because the composites are found deeper, they tend to carry a higher concentration of hydrogen sulfide. This needs to be handled as well. This is where MPLX enters the picture.

From the last 10-K, it’s already visible that MPLX is doing this exact thing right now, namely addressing the increase in sour gas produced. The “Southwest” is the reporting segment for the Delaware Basin, and between 2023 and 2025, the throughput growth has been 33.3%. Overall throughput growth was 19% meaning that this segment is doing a lot of the heavy lifting. But this is exactly what I’d like to see from MPLX. But because of the notion of a “sour gas premium” I see MPLX not only raising the throughput volumes but also the EBITDA margins as a result. The “sweeter” gas is a game of numbers really, this more acidic gas is more about specialty treatment. The same fees aren’t applicable. For anyone reading about companies looking at expanding into these types of formations, they will likely find that their cost per barrel will be higher than what the legacy formations have produced because of these additional costs associated.

It would be extremely illegal for companies to put this gas into regular interstate pipelines, that is a disaster waiting to happen as it would corrode the metal much, much faster. The Titan Complex that MPLX is expanding will be fully operational in Q4 this year adding 440 MMcf/d in treating capacity. This adds 23.1% processed volume growth in the Southwest segment. The Titan Complex was the result of a previous acquisition of Northwind last year for $2.375 billion in total.

Looking back at the past 10 years, MPLX has made long strides to improve its cash ROIC. It looked quite shaky a few years ago when the cash position was near nonexistent, but through quite aggressive debt funding it was able to raise the volumes. This has led to the company not posting a year with YoY declines in adjusted EBITDA. That is quite impressive, I think.

MPLX does have a considerable debt pile for an MLP with a market cap of $56 billion. Why did it happen between 2024 and 2025? Well, I’d like to point to the invested capital figure, which grew by $4.7 billion. This came largely from new debt that MPLX issued. Historically, MPLX has had a history of issuing units as well as a way to raise capital. Since 2016 the unit count has gone from 338 million to 1.019 billion. It peaked in 2020 and has been declining slightly since.

The transaction is expected to be immediately accretive to distributable cash flow and represents a 7x multiple on forecast 2027 EBITDA.

The reason I bring this up in relation to the Northwind acquisition is because of the multiple MPLX paid, namely 7x 2027 EBITDA. This captures an ROIC of 14.28% (1/7 = 14.28%). In 2024, MPLX issued $1.65 billion in debt at 5.5%, then in 2025, it issued more at rates of 5.4% and 5.9%. The point here is that even though MPLX is supposedly locking in a lower ROIC with the acquisition of Northwind at 14.28% compared to its consolidated cash ROIC, the net spread is still 8.8–9%. This is exactly the type of leverage that will help ensure the unit distributions can grow at a steady pace over the long term.

Quick Look At The Valuation

On a valuation basis, looking at just EBITDA, MPLX doesn't look like the most compelling buy at the moment. When using the adjusted EBITDA figure of 2025, the TTM multiple would be 11.54. This seems far more reasonable I think. Management does not seem to have guided for a specific figure in terms of 2026 adj. EBITDA. Using the 10-year average compound rate of 49.4% would be a far stretch, to say the least. The past 3 years seem far better to look at, which is 3.83% per year. This puts 2026 adj. EBITDA at $7.289 billion, and a FWD EV/EBITDA of 11.09x. This is still at a pretty steep premium to the sector median, and above what MPLX also paid for their Northwind acquisition.

Considering the incredibly strong ROIC that it has been able to report, along with several key market tailwinds that could very well accelerate the adj. EBITDA above 3.83% means that MPLX is still a strong buy in my book.

Risks

People pointing to commodities being a main risk haven't really looked at the last 10 years of adjusted EBITDA growth for MPLX and compared that to how commodity prices have shifted. The correlation might be found in other parts of the financial statement, but for the core performance and earnings, I don’t think it's there.

Rather, the main risk that MPLX does face is a large reliance on MPC. Seeing as the logistics and storage operations are overwhelmingly dependent on it, it means that any negative development in terms of deal-making would have severe effects on the bottom line. It’s not that MPLX is held hostage by MPC, but it’s also not that they are entirely free either. But I guess you could find this same scenario with a lot of other companies as well.

The second issue relates to some of the trends in the Permian at the moment. There is a lot of consolidation at the top level. Companies are being bought out, and even if the overall volumes are rising, the priority right now seems to be far more about capital discipline than drilling at all costs and raising volumes while risking posting lower margins. This is where midstream companies get affected because they rely on strong or at least some production volume growth to grow their own top and bottom line.

The Full Picture

MPLX continues to be one of my top picks in the midstream industry right now and with a compelling yield of nearly 8% and a fantastic internal cash ROIC of 18.2% with plenty of tailwinds in the Delaware Basin, MPLX only makes sense as a strong buy now. It’s been a while since the last time I wrote about it, so it was about time to do an update and I’m glad to say I’m as bullish as I was then, or even more so after a strong FY2025 report.

data.title: Brown-Forman: Stuck In The Middle Of A Potential Buyout And Rising Macro Pressure
data.description:
Introduction

Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.A) (BF.B) recently fell to attractive levels following a continued decline over the past half a decade, recently rising on the news of a potential buyout.

Although the business quality remains high, the portfolio is attractive, and the balance sheet is healthy, Brown-Forman is a Hold, as the stock is trading somewhere in the middle of what I consider a fair valuation for this business and the levels we might see them getting bought at, with rising risks of volatility in case no deal is reached alongside the Iran-driven pressure.

Navigating a Tough Environment

Brown-Forman's Q3'FY26 and YTD performance was strong overall, beating the market's expectations and navigating the ongoing macro headwinds such as lower used barrel sales and American products remaining off the shelf in Canada (where reported sales are down 60% on a reported basis due to the Trump-related boycott; not that big for them on a global scale), while advancing in emerging markets and reporting double-digit growth in their New Mix/innovation portfolio, reporting $628 million in free cash flow during the 9M period (vs. $329 in 9M'FY25) as a result of falling CAPEX and working capital tailwinds.

Following this report, the company remains on track to meet their guidance, with their portfolio optimizations as they focus on the growth segment, aiming at an organic net sales and operating income decline in the low-single-digit range, with CAPEX of $110 million to $120 million.

Recently, Brown-Forman was reportedly the target of a potential acquisition, with Sazerac reportedly competing with Pernod Ricard (PDRDF) in a deal that a TD Cowen analyst stated could lead to a price of $33 to $34 at the time, with the Sazerac offer reportedly landing at $15 billion, meaning about $32 per share.

Unless you bought this shortly before the initial reports came out, this would be a terrible deal for a vast majority of buyers seen in recent years, as it would lock them out on a loss that can be quite significant if we're looking at the >$80 we saw back in 2020-2021.

However, this highlights interest in the company's core operations (at least), with the potential to see an activist investor step in and call for an accelerated restructuring and focus on preparing the company for a potential macro recovery.

Financially, based on Brown-Forman's latest report, we can see a very strong position, with the current assets covering their total liabilities, with a manageable amount of debt and a strong $444 million in cash and cash equivalents, further emphasizing why this can be attractive for a buyer.

This also provides flexibility regarding the company's cash flow, buying $400 million worth of stock and paying $321 million in dividends over the 9M period, for a dividend yield of about 3.17% alongside ~3.70% in buybacks based on the amount spent so far.

Brown-Forman's next earnings report is scheduled for June 3, being expected to report $0.32 in EPS and $879.16 million in revenue. I will pay close attention to their guidance for the next fiscal year, and even though I don't expect the overall tone/results to be that great given the ongoing macro pressure, the company could give more insights into the offers unless something is confirmed by that time, which can generate even more attention from the market.

Falling Volumes, Different Reasons

Although we see the market worry about consumption, especially in recent years, I see this as a ramification of the premiumization trend that's been going on for several decades, as more options, niches, and demands for better quality over volume take over the industry.

This has been affected by the weakness seen in the consumer environment since the pandemic, which translated into trade-downs, budget constraints, weaker levels of confidence, and falling discretionary budgets, and I believe the market is mistaking affordability issues that have been reported for virtually everything else in recent years for fundamental changes in consumption.

It's not that much about people simply drinking less or the "alcohol recession" highlighted by some, but premiumization (drinking less but being willing to spend more on quality/the experience) and specialization (companies going for various niches beyond classic alcohol), on the other hand, are real fundamental trends.

Still, unlike companies focusing mostly on low-quality beer, for example, Brown-Forman is ready for something like this, especially if they continue their expansion into RTD and focus on emerging markets. The fact that we saw these reports of potential buyers being interested in the company further reinforces that big money is aware of their potential and future-facing portfolio and sees profits here, especially at current prices.

However, the Iran conflict can certainly add even more pressure on this, as we're talking about something that looks like much more than a "temporary" boost in pump prices, with the region playing an important role for vital commodities such as oil, gas, fertilizers, and other derivatives, which can lead to more inflation even if the conflict were to end today.

So far, the Strait of Hormuz is closed (again), and the latest development is Trump threatening to blow up the country's infrastructure after Iran's "Total Violation of our Ceasefire Agreement" following the attack on a ship trying to pass through, justifying it by the US' blockade on their ports despite the agreement.

As a result, the war premiums can last for longer than the market expects, resulting in even more inflationary pressure that can not only result in more pressure on the already weak consumer environment but also a pause in rate cuts or even hikes, pushing borrowing costs higher and affecting people and companies alike.

Risks Reiterated

Regardless of what factor the market believes is behind it, the fact is that these companies have been performing expectably weak during the past few years, which clearly affected their valuation.

Even though I believe we're talking about temporary issues due to the pressure caused by the high-rate environment and subsequent events (e.g., tariffs, Iran), given the ongoing macroenvironment and re-escalation in Iran, the outlook is getting worse and worse, potentially leading to more pressure on the company, its consumers, and the stock.

Valuation

For Brown-Forman's DCF to Equity valuation, assuming no change in net borrowings given their strong balance sheet, I'll assume $750 million in FCF in FY26 given their strong performance over the 9M period and the guidance, but will assume this falls to $650 million in FY27 as OCF would normalize.

This would be followed by a 5% CAGR until FY30 to assume continued growth in RTD and emerging markets alongside a potential recovery in developed markets during this time, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with an 8% discount rate to demand an appropriate margin of safety from a company like this, with 462.86 million shares outstanding as of their Q3'FY26 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $12.80 billion, for an intrinsic value of $27.66, which is similar to the $29.15 at the moment of writing this, so we can see how the market is pricing in the M&A potential for now.

Conclusion

Brown-Forman is a Hold, as the stock is trading somewhere in the middle of a fair value and the levels we might see them getting bought at. However, that also comes with the potential to see more pressure in case they don't reach a deal, with rising risks from the Iran conflict, potential for more tariff pressure, and the inflationary wave that could slow them down further.

data.title: Polaris Global Equity Composite Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
2026 started out well, with global growth tracking slightly above expectations. The U.S. Federal Reserve held its benchmark rate at 3.5%–3.75% following a December cut, and several other G20 central banks maintained or reduced rates, creating a broadly supportive macro backdrop through January and February. Cyclicals drove strong early returns, even as U.S. tariff threats continued. March brought a decisive turn. The war in Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz sent energy prices sharply higher. Both the European Central Bank and Bank of Japan held rates steady but cautioned that the ongoing conflict poses risks of rising inflation and slowing economic growth. The pivot reshuffled sector leadership — lifting energy and defensive positions while tamping down AI-adjacent cyclicals.

On this backdrop, the Polaris Global Equity Composite gained 5.91% (net of fees), outperforming the MSCI World Index which declined -3.47%. The Composite outperformed in most sectors including double-digit gains from energy, information technology (IT), materials and utilities. Absolute detractors included consumer discretionary and communication services; however, the Composite still outperformed the benchmark in both sectors. Notwithstanding a significant underweight relative to the benchmark, the United States was a notable source of strength. Other regions of similarly strong gains included Norway, Canada, Italy, Netherlands and off-benchmark regions such as South Korea. China declined on its own domestic challenges (constrained manufacturing, lower consumption), while a handful of U.K. stocks in unrelated industries (Nomad Foods (NOMD), International Consolidated Airlines (ICAGY)) tempered results.

First Quarter 2026 Performance Analysis

Energy stocks were among the best contributors to performance in absolute terms. ENI SpA (E), Marathon Petroleum Corp. (MPC), and TotalEnergies SE (TTE) all advanced strongly as the market repriced energy businesses in response to the Strait of Hormuz closure and surging oil prices. Both ENI and TotalEnergies entered the period having reported robust 2025 annual earnings, driven by strong operational performance and production growth. Both companies have focused on strong cash generation and ongoing shareholder returns, reinforcing investor willingness to hold in a heated commodity market.

Stocks within the IT sector had a strong start to the year, driven by the AI boom creating genuine shortages in advanced computer chips and memory. SK hynix Inc. controls 57%+ of the high bandwidth memory (HBM) market, and its entire 2026 HBM production is already sold out. Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) confirmed its next-generation HBM4 chips are on track for delivery to NVIDIA (NVDA) in early 2026. U.S.-based MKS Inc. (MKSI) had a record year for wafer fab equipment orders, with double-digit sales across semiconductor, advanced packaging and photonics end markets. Arrow Electronics (ARW) beat consensus as the electronics distribution industry recovered from the 2023–2024 inventory correction, with stabilizing orders and improving lead times. We exited Capgemini SE (CAPMF), a French tech consulting firm, after growing concern that its corporate clients would handle more IT work inhouse rather than outsource it.

When the Strait of Hormuz closed, it disrupted roughly 30% of the world's nitrogen supply — a key ingredient in fertilizer — sending prices higher. Norwegian fertilizer producer Yara International (YARIY) was largely insulated from the disruption, and picked up market share from competitors who couldn't deliver. Methanex Corp. (MEOH) was another strong contributor in the materials sector, benefiting from supply disruptions on two fronts — first from natural gas shortages, then from the Hormuz closure — both of which pushed methanol prices higher. Crucially, the company had more product to sell at those elevated prices, having added over 20% to its global production capacity through the 2025 acquisition of OCI Global's methanol business. More volume at higher prices proved to be a powerful combination, and we sold the position at a healthy profit having reached our valuation target. Lundin Mining (LUNMF) capitalized on tight copper supply and strong performance at its Caserones mine, while the Vicuna joint venture bolstered the long-term growth outlook.

Within the healthcare sector, Gilead Sciences (GILD) gained more than 14% after publishing promising cancer treatment results, and expanding its oncology franchise through a partnership with Kymera Therapeutics (KYMR). United Therapeutics Corp. (UTHR) rebounded at the very end of March after announcing that its TETON-1 pivotal study of nebulized Tyvaso met its primary endpoint in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. Lantheus Holdings Inc. (LNTH) gained after earnings beat expectations and the FDA approved a new formulation for its PSMA PET imaging agent, Pylarify. Elevance Health (ELV) shares fell as the company faced a confluence of headwinds, including a regulatory threat from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services over its Medicare Advantage enrollment practices and a weaker-than-expected 2026 profit outlook.

The Composite's holdings in financials held up better than the benchmark's sector returns. Double-digit gains from Webster Financial (WBS) and DNB Bank (DNBBY) were largely offset by declines from Capital One Financial (COF), SLM Corp. (SLM) and Ping An Insurance Group (PNGAY). Spanish conglomerate Banco Santander (SAN) made a play for Webster, paying out $75 per share of WBS, a 14% premium to the pre-announcement price. DNB stock ticked higher, driven by strong earnings, a robust Norwegian economy, and upward analyst revisions. SLM Corp. had a volatile quarter: shares faced pressure in February on modest increases in early-stage delinquencies and labor market softening, but rebounded in March after the company priced its first student-loan ABS transaction of the year and announced a $200 million accelerated share repurchase. Ping An Insurance Group underperformed as a proxy for China macro pessimism, while Capital One Financial fell sharply in January after earnings missed consensus estimates, with net income declining 51% year over year largely due to Discover acquisition costs.

Industrials had classic barbell returns, with Marubeni Corp. (MARUY) and Allison Transmission Holdings (ALSN) among the top 15 contributors, while International Consolidated Airlines and Teleperformance (TLPFF) were laggards. Marubeni Corp. offered an upward revision to its full-year profit forecast on a bullish stance for copper prices, guiding for increasing annual dividends and committing to an additional share buyback. Allison Transmission issued an upbeat financial outlook for the year, underpinned by the completed acquisition of Dana's off-highway segment. Adding to the momentum, Allison reported stronger production schedules from key partners, including General Motors (GM). International Consolidated Airlines Group faced pressure in March as oil's sharp move and the "risk-off" tone hit economically-sensitive transport names simultaneously. Teleperformance faced persistent investor fears that generative AI will structurally disrupt the customer service outsourcing industry.

Same theme, different industry: French communication services company Publicis Groupe (PUBGY) entered 2026 ranked #1 in 2025 global new-business performance; however, the company declined as it could not shake investor fears that generative AI will structurally erode the profitability of traditional advertising agency models.

Within consumer discretionary stocks, Kia Corp. (KIMTF) was a consistent bright spot, as solid U.S. sales, aggressive growth guidance, and enthusiasm around its hybrid EV expansion, robotics, and AI positioning helped the stock look past near-term tariff concerns. Record 2025 annual sales in both the U.S. and India, led by SUV and hybrid demand, bolstered the outlook. Offsetting this, Alibaba Group (BABA) struggled as investors questioned the return on investment from its $52 billion AI and cloud infrastructure commitment through 2027. Sony Group (SONY) also declined, pressured by memory chip price spikes that threatened PlayStation 5 margins and the delayed release of several popular software titles. Over the course of the quarter, we exited four positions and initiated two new ones, trimming company exposure that either reached our target valuation level (Methanex Corp., Sally Beauty Holdings (SBH)) or no longer supported our original theses (UnitedHealth Group and Capgemini SE), while adding to industrial and materials names with near-term catalysts. On the buy side, we initiated a position in Eastman Chemical (EMN), a specialty chemical company that has streamlined its business and cut costs significantly. We also added Ryanair Holdings (RYAAY), Europe's largest budget airline and a name we previously owned during the pandemic. The oil-driven selloff pushed the stock to a price we found attractive, and while higher fuel costs are a headwind for all airlines, Ryanair's structural cost advantages and 80% hedged fuel costs give it more room to absorb the pressure than U.S. full-service carriers. With European air travel supply still tight relative to demand, we see Ryanair as well positioned to keep taking customers from weaker competitors.

Investment Environment And Strategy

The Middle East conflict and the Strait of Hormuz closure have introduced a level of volatility that is unlikely to resolve quickly. Oil prices at current levels are not sustainable for a global economy that is already soft in many regions, and the prospect of rate cuts — which markets were counting on — has effectively been taken off the table. That said, not everything about this environment works against us. Higher rates, while a drag on growth, tend to benefit our financial holdings and discourage speculative investing that has contributed to growth dominance over the past few years. We will continue to look selectively for opportunities where volatility creates dislocations, adding quality names at attractive valuations.

The more fundamental shift — and the one we believe has the most lasting significance — is the growing recognition that owning only U.S. stocks is no longer a winning strategy. For the better part of 18 months, geopolitical stress, dollar uncertainty, and uneven global growth have been quietly building the case for international diversification. That case is now impossible to ignore. Investors with a heavy U.S. bias are increasingly wary; the instinct to look beyond American borders is accelerating. This is precisely where we have been positioned, and we believe a genuinely global portfolio is as well-suited to the current environment as any we have seen in years.

data.title: Sify Technologies: All Eyes On Proposed Data Center Segment IPO - Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Sify Technologies Limited, or “Sify” (NASDAQ:SIFY), previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Almost two years ago, I outlined an opportunity for speculative investors to benefit from a highly dilutive and heavily discounted rights offering conducted by Indian ICT solutions provider Sify Technologies.

While the idea played out handsomely, I had no interest in owning the company's American Depository Shares ("ADS") for the long-term due to the company's poor track record and the fact that entities affiliated with CEO and Chairman Raju Vegesna control approximately 84% of the company's common shares and ADS.

Unfortunately, I underestimated the power of the ongoing AI data center hype as evidenced by the epic rally in the ADS over the past year:

At the beginning of last year, the company announced a $5 billion investment plan aimed at expanding the company’s data center footprint, modernizing internal systems and acquiring GPUs until the end of the decade.

Six months ago, reports emerged of a partnership with Meta Platforms (META) for a massive 500MW data center in the state of Andhra Pradesh.

In an attempt to capitalize on the hype and raise funds for its ambitious investment plan, the company is preparing an IPO for its data center unit Sify Infinit Spaces Limited or "SISL" which currently commands 188MW of data center capacity.

Reportedly, the company is looking to raise approximately $270 million at a targeted valuation of $4.2 billion with pre-IPO investors expected to sell shares worth approximately $130 million at current exchange rates.

While a valuation of approximately 20x annual revenues and up to 40x EV/EBITDA appears aggressive, I would expect the IPO to see huge demand from institutional and retail investors, particularly with the AI infrastructure hype raging at full force again.

In recent years, the data center segment has become Sify's main growth and profitability driver. Please note that all figures are in million USD based on a conversion rate of 83 INR per USD:

While SISL's Draft Red Herring Prospectus ("DRHP") was approved by the Securities and Exchange Board of India ("SEBI") in January, recent market volatility has resulted in the company waiting for a more "conducive market environment based on bankers' guidance" as outlined by management on last week's conference call.

However, with AI infrastructure plays being in high demand again and the Middle East conflict being more of a tailwind for the company as customers in the GCC region are looking for alternatives, I would expect the IPO to commence sooner rather than later.

Please note that under the scenario outlined above, Sify's post-IPO stake in SISL would be approximately 83.1% with a calculated value of $3.5 billion.

But even after the recent rally, Sify's market capitalization remains below $1 billion. While a holding company discount appears warranted (particularly with Sify being controlled by its CEO and Chairman), a 70%+ valuation gap seems excessive.

While a holding company discount of 50% would still be on the high side, upside in Sify's ADS would be substantially. Should the SISL IPO be indeed met with enthusiasm, upside could be even higher.

Using the targeted SISL IPO valuation and applying a holding company discount of 50% would yield a $24 price target or almost 80% upside for Sify's ADS.

Consequently, I am raising my rating from "Strong Sell" to "Buy".

Risk Factors

As usual, the bull case is not without risks. Should the SISL IPO experience further delays or fetch a significantly lower valuation, Sify's ADS price is likely to decline.

Bottom Line

The ongoing AI infrastructure hype has propelled Sify Technologies' ADS to new multi-year highs with investors eagerly awaiting the IPO of the company's data center subsidiary Sify Infinit Spaces.

A successful IPO would likely reduce Sify's current holding company discount quite meaningfully. As a result, I am upgrading the company's ADS from "Strong Sell" to "Buy" with a price target of $24.

data.title: Marvell: An AI Winner Tackling The Biggest Bottleneck With Data Transfer Optics
data.description:
Marvell Technology (MRVL) has gained nearly 60% since my last report, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 6.6% gain. In October 2025, I initiated coverage with a $119.47 price target. With that price target reached, it is a good moment to update my assessment on the stock, as I believe the stock has substantially more growth ahead, driven by AI growth and its growth acceleration in optics data transfer solutions.

Marvell Technology Provides Connectivity And Custom Silicon Solutions

Marvell Technology is different from companies such as Nvidia and AMD. The latter companies provide general-purpose graphic processing units. Demand for those is high, but we also note that in the scaling phase of AI, there is high demand for ASICs as well. ASICs provide higher performance per watt, but they are specifically designed for one workload. So, AI application needs custom silicon as well as GPUs.

What we are increasingly seeing is that with GPUs becoming more powerful, the need for higher data storage and transfer systems is also increasing. In 2020, Marvell acquired Inphi, providing the company with rack-to-rack data transfer capabilities by combining electrical signals with optical signals at the edge. In February 2026, Marvell acquired Celestial AI for $1 billion plus 27 million shares. The acquisition provides Marvell with high-speed optical solutions inside chips, but also on the board level and, in the future, on the rack-to-rack level. That is basically a high-speed neural network.

There are, of course, risks for Marvell Technology apart from the obvious opportunities. In the XPU (meaning GPUs plus custom ASICs). The AI CapEx allocation between custom silicon and GPUs is a risk. We also note that in the custom silicon part of the market, Broadcom (AVGO) is the dominant player.

For data processing units, Broadcom and Nvidia are competitors, while Broadcom, Intel, and Cisco are competing on optical data transfer solutions.

Wall Street Expectations For Marvell Technology In Q1 2027

Marvell Technology will report Q1 2027 results on the 28th of May. Analysts are currently expecting the company to book $2.4 billion in revenues, indicating nearly 27% growth. We note that over the past 3 and 6 months, the revenue estimates have increased by 5.2% and 10.5%.

Earnings per share are expected to grow by 28.3% to $0.80, with estimates having increased by 1.8% and 5.7% over the past three and six months. We note that the company’s track record on beating analyst estimates is good, with 10 out of 12 quarters showing a beat on revenues and EPS.

The Celestial AI acquisition will not have material revenue contributions until the second half of FY28, when the company expects revenues to ramp up to a $500 million annualized run rate, with doubling run rate revenues a year later.

Marvell Technology Stock Is A Buy On Optics Growth

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Marvell Technology, I had a price target of $119.47 based on expected FY26 earnings. Typically, we assess the price target based on expected earnings one year ahead, which would be FY28 for Marvell. That would indicate 24% upside in the base case when valuing the stock in line with peers and brings the price target to $176.63. Against the company median EV/EBITDA, we would get to a $218.57 price target, indicating 56% upside. We also note that the stock is currently closely valued to its expected FY27 earnings with around 6% upside, which provides some support for the 56% upside target being the more optimistic target but not unrealistic.

The growth metrics are impressive for Marvell, with around 32% in annual growth on sales and EBITDA and 49% growth on free cash flow.

The margins, look like they are fluctuating quite a bit, and that is driven by the acquisition of Celestial AI. Once revenues are ramped up, we see that the margins are closer to 40% again. For this year, the shareholder returns are somewhat pressured in the sense that potential share repurchases are merely offsetting the shares issued for the Celestial AI acquisition. Capital expenditures are set to decline from 6% of revenues in FY24 to 3% by FY29. We do note that free cash flow is set to substantially increase this year and that supports additional share repurchases in the years ahead, as the company has a comfortable net debt leverage. Conclusion: Marvell Technology Remains An Appealing Buy

Marvell Technology stock has risen 64% year-to-date, and it may seem like after such a big run-up in the stock price, there may be little upside left. However, I do believe that Marvell Technology still holds 24%-56% upside with compelling growth in the years ahead. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating.

data.title: TSMC: AI Supercycle Buys Don't Come Stronger Than This
data.description:
Share prices of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) or TSMC have risen 8.2% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500. Earlier in April, the company reported financial results that exceeded estimates by more than $400 million on revenues and by $0.16 on earnings per share. In this report, I discuss TSMC’s Q1 2026 earnings, the outlook for 2026, and an update to my price target.

TSMC Puts Efficiency On Display As Capacity Is Constrained

TSM’s revenues grew 6.4% sequentially and 40.6% to $35.9 billion. Gross margins increased 3.9 points sequentially and 7.4 points to 66.2%. This is driven by the ramp-up of 3nm node sales. I believe this is a very strong result, given that geographical diversification is compressing the margins somewhat. That geographical margin headwind is not only driven by learning curves and production ramp-ups, but those international facilities have a lower mature gross margin as they are simply not as optimized as the facilities and logistics in Taiwan. Operating margins increased 4.1 points sequentially and 9.6 points year-on-year to 58.1%. Equivalent wafer shipments grew 5.4% sequentially and 28.1% year-on-year. It shows that the growth in revenues is not solely driven by volumes, but also by a better revenue mix.

The sales in the 7nm and below category account for 74% of all sales. Within that category, we see that the 7nm and 5nm node shares declined by 3 points to 17% and 49%, while the 3nm node sales grew to 34% of the 7nm or smaller category. In the prior quarter, we already saw a substantial increase in the sales share, and that continued in Q1. The company already expected the 3nm node margins to be accretive in 2026 and has now further detailed this to the second half of 2026 with opportunities to convert some production of higher nodes to the 3nm production.

Sequentially, we saw that the main drivers of sales were high-performance computing and the Internet of Things. Smartphones were down 11%, which I believe is largely seasonal and also reflects some softness due to high memory prices. Automotive was down 7%, while digital consumer electronics was up 28%. We note that over 90% of sales is generated in HPC, smartphones, and the Internet of Things.

Q2 Guidance Implies Double Digit Growth

For the second quarter, TSM is guiding for sales between $39 billion and $40.2 billion, which would indicate 8.6%-12% growth, with the midpoint at 10.3%. We see that gross profits are expected to be between 65.5% and 67.5% and operating margins between 56.5% and 58.5%. For TSM, the ramp-up of 3nm will bring higher average sales prices for the entire revenue mix, and as the 3nm production ramps up, the margins will also become better, which should reduce its impact on composite margins and even be accretive to margins later this year.

The company expects more than 30% growth in revenues this year. The 3nm node demand is extremely strong. That also requires the production capacity to be expanded, with the new N3 fab in Tainan expected to come online in H1 2027, production in Arizona for Fab 2 will use N3 in H2 2027, and the Fab 2 in Japan is planned for 2028. So, as the margins mature and most of the learning on the production is done, we see that TSM is increasing the global capacity. We note that a 2nm ramp-up will pressure margins somewhat. But overall, we are seeing a healthy plan being executed for 3nm and an eye on the future with the 2nm node ramp-up and the company addressing the packaging bottleneck with higher investments.

TSM Estimates Increased And Stock Remains Attractive

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For TSM, I am slightly adjusting the way the price target is determined. Previously, I used the peer group as a reference to set the price target. I am now using the company’s 3-year median EV/EBITDA. This would indicate 26% upside with a $467.34 price target. We also see that the company is currently trading in line with its 2026 earnings. EBITDA estimates for 2026 and 2027 have increased by 10.4% and by 3.3% on EBITDA. Sales are expected to grow 27% annually through 2028, with 29% growth in EBITDA and 23% growth in free cash flow. Given the company’s upside, the undervaluation against the peer group, and the net cash position, I am marking the stock a strong buy.

This year, the EBITDA margin is expected to climb substantially, driven by the 3nm node production ramp-up, which will be accretive to margins. In 2027, the margin is expected to drop somewhat as the 2nm node production is expected to ramp up, and internationally we will see 3nm node production capacity being added. In the years ahead, we see that free cash flow will grow materially. At the same time, we note that there is a CapEx step-up that will pressure the free cash flow generation somewhat, and that will be most notable this year as the company steps up capital expenditures for increasing capacity and addressing packaging bottlenecks. In terms of leverage, there is really no concern, given that the company is expected to grow out its net cash position in the years ahead with potentially $190 billion in cash on the balance sheet by 2028, giving the company a lot of space to continue building an edge in semiconductor manufacturing, and there would even be space for share repurchases, although the company is not really known for going big on repurchases.

Conclusion: TSMC’s Story Is Getting Stronger

While we are seeing some concerns in the stock market about the AI investment cycle and mostly about the return on those investments, we are seeing TSMC’s business growing, and that is actually not that much different from what we see at ASML, which enables the manufacturing of advanced chips. My rating on TSM remains a strong buy as we see the company ramping up its 3nm node with improving margin performance while the company is also comfortably investing for added capacity and new technology ramp-ups.
