data.title: The Top 10 BDCs: Which Is The Best Buy?
data.description:
Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction

I haven't owned any BDC since September 2024, when Fed Chair Powell started the Fed's rate cut cycle.

At the time, we held both Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), which we had owned since 2021. While we enjoyed the high yields, I feared that a lower rate regime would lead the floating rate coupons to reset lower, NII per share to compress, and multiples on BDCs to compress.

At the time I said:

These BDC's thrive in a higher for longer rate environment, and this is about to end. Both have cut below their 200 day SMA, and we could be seeing a lot more bearishness soon.

Now I know a lot of investors who rely on income have BDCs in their portfolios to juice the yield they derive.

But with Moody's having cut the sector outlook to negative a couple weeks ago on April 7th, I feel like it's an interesting time to revisit the sector and provide some guidance.

In the past year, the sector has been making headlines with the First Brands Group fraud and bankruptcy, which hit multiple BDCs, and then the Tricolor Fallout, which adds to sector stress.

The timeline above sums up the stress events that have occurred to the sector since I stopped tracking BDCs.

In this article, I'm going to take a holistic look at available BDCs and answer the question: if I had to own a BDC, which one would it be?

The Framework: How to Pick a Single BDC

These intellectual exercises require setting a framework for how to discard picks gradually until you're left with just one candidate.

For BDCs, the approach differs as these are credit businesses. I'll be looking at the following factors to make a decision:

  • Scale and diversification: the size of the portfolio, the number of loans
  • Credit quality: non-accruals, % of PIK, the trajectory.
  • Mark conservatism: how a loan is marked relative to the same loan at another BDC
  • Quality of the parent company
  • Balance sheet: is it investment grade? What's the leverage?, etc.
  • Dividend durability: what's the coverage, the history of dividend cuts?
  • Valuation
  • Cyclical positioning: how well is it positioned to survive two or three more bad quarters?

The List of BDCs That I Will Look at

I'll be looking at the following 10 BDCs in this article, which are the 10 largest publicly traded vehicles. Picking the largest 10 already tilts us towards platforms with scale, which is one of the factors used in evaluating these opportunities.

I have compiled a dataset of 20-30 BDCs, which I will use as a reference for mark data.

These are the 10 that make the elimination round.

  • Ares Capital
  • Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL)
  • Blue Owl Capital Corporation
  • Main Street Capital (MAIN)
  • Golub Capital BDC (GBDC)
  • Hercules Capital (HTGC)
  • Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX)
  • FS KKR Capital (FSK)
  • Capital Southwest (CSWC)
  • Prospect Capital (PSEC)

A First Round of Pruning

Off the bat there are quite a few that can be discarded.

Prospect Capital is out. The BDC has been destroying cash, and the dividend has already been cut twice in the past ten years.

Net asset value has been trending in the wrong direction. If you're going to own a single BDC, you can't touch something this toxic.

Next, FS KKR is also out.

Sure, the KKR merger did work to clean up an ugly book, but this portfolio carries more toxic loans than any of the other options.

It is the only BDC in the universe that has such a high non-accrual rate.

As you can see, the non-accruals as a percentage of cost are usually higher because, by the time they are non-accrual, the value of the loans has been written down, reducing their share of the book at fair value.

On cost, FSK's non-accruals run in the 5.5% range.

Quick terminology reminder: a non-accrual loan is one that is no longer generating interest for owners, as the borrower has stopped paying.

This is not a candidate for an "only one BDC" pick.

Finally, I'll drop Hercules Capital from the list. It's not bad per se; only the venture lending BDC model is different from the sponsor-finance BDC model, which makes it a non-apples-to-apples comparison, and it makes my life easier to just drop it from the debate.

We're now left with 7 for a second round of elimination.

Round 2: Expensive Names and Narrow Scale

If we're going to pick a BDC in an environment in which Moody's has given an outright negative outlook for the industry, we want to think twice about paying a premium for assets.

Here's the logic:

  • higher quality books do deserve a premium to NAV.
  • but amid entire industry stress, the premium is at risk.
  • even if the book does relatively better, there is more room for compression.

So while I'm fully ready to be heckled, because of this concern I will start by removing Main Street Capital from the list.

Yes, it has a clean book, and less SaaS exposure (under 10% vs. 20% sector average), but it trades at a premium both to its own history and to the peer set.

To be clear, MAIN is internally managed, which justifies a premium of, say, 15-20% over externally managed platforms just because of the structuring of the cash flows that aren't encumbered with fees.

And yes, MAIN is not a pure BDC in that all of its income isn't debt-derived. It has an equity allocation that has compounded from $12 to over $33 since 2007, driving shareholder value. Discounting cash flows suggests that a premium should be applied if we expect the denominator to grow.

Many BDCs have seen their NAVs remain flat, whilst MAIN has been growing.

But there need to be limits to this. To start, its non-accruals are not the lowest; it only yields 5.7%, which for a BDC is on the lower side, and the 64% premium to NAV becomes difficult to justify in the current environment.

So it won't be MAIN.

A similar argument is valid for Capital Southwest.

While it offers an attractive 10.5% dividend yield, is internally managed, and has been a good dividend payer in the past, it is a smaller player and is less diversified than some of its peers. It currently trades at a 45% premium to NAV, which is an opportunity for compression should the sector continue to deliver negative surprises.

Sixth Street Specialty Lending is also in this bucket, although to a lesser extent.

Sixth Street is a well-recognized credit franchise, and it's a conservative lender that writes down its book earlier and more often than its peers. It yields 10% and is actually a serious contender for a multi-BDC book. But if we're looking at only one, I don't believe the internally managed premium will hold across the board in a difficult environment.

It's also a relatively smaller platform at $3.4bn and less diversified than other options.

This now leaves us with 4 names.

The Final Four

At this point we're left with 4 of the 5 largest BDCs by portfolio size.

Ares, Blue Owl Capital Corporation, Blackstone Secured Lending, and Golub Capital BDC.

These also happen to be the top 4 by portfolio company count. (Of course these two metrics are correlated)

I'll remove OBDC from the list. The Blue Owl platform has made headlines because of its exposure to First Brands. In the analysis of all of the loans available across 20+ BDCs, I found 52 loans that were shared by different BDCs. (I might have missed a bunch; I reconstituted data from 10-Ks and 10-Qs).

OBDC found itself often having loans on its books that it rated higher than peers.

I find that mark conservatism is an interesting metric that nobody is highlighting.

But when you have exposure to the same loan across 5 BDCs, and one marks it at 70% of cost, whereas others are marking it at 100% of cost, you might ask if the 100% one is being overly optimistic. Especially when this shows up as a pattern.

This isn't a perfect indicator, as it's a mixed bag with OBDC.

This leaves us with three picks: ARCC, GBDC, and BXSL. The 3 lowest non-accrual percentages across the peer set.

And honestly, if you were going to take on BDC risk, why not build smaller positions of all 3.

But I promised this article was "if I had to buy only one", so we have to prune one way or another.

Now, of course, the lazy move would be to just say, Buy ARCC. It has been through the GFC and 2020 without resetting its dividend. It's the largest platform and has the highest number of portfolio companies.

A closer look shows that on a price-to-NAV basis, GBDC and BXSL take it. BXSL wins on non-accruals.

When it comes to mark conservatism, ARCC wins, and this is quite meaningful.

Unfortunately for this exercise, we only get a handful of marks to get a signal from, and GBDC should be excluded as it only has 3 loans with peer comparables from my collection.

ARCC systematically has the most conservative marks vs. peer mean on the set, whereas BXSL consistently has more aggressive marks.

BXSL isn't proven through a crisis, and when you look at positions per billion dollars of book, it is not as well diversified as GBDC.

This leaves GBDC vs. ARCC.

And here it is not easy to decide, but I think that my gut says ARCC is the BDC you want.

GBDC recently cut its dividend, which is management being proactive.

They have also indicated that the outlook is negative and 2026 will be a tough year.

ARCC has a better track record, an equivalent 10% yield, and its scale gives it better sourcing. It has the largest size yet had no exposure to First Brands or Tricolor.

The hard data just points towards the obvious pick here.

And my extra research on BDC marks, while limited, pointed to ARCC as being the cleanest, most conservative marker, which means that it writes down bad loans before peers do.

The chart above shows the distribution of the highest mark vs. the lowest mark.

Some have wild variations, and ARCC is consistently more conservative than its peers.

It's investment grade across all three rating agencies, and its dividend has some cushion even if NII dips.

So ARCC is a Buy?

This is the point in the article where I disappoint readers who were looking for a "Buy this BDC" recommendation.

At best, ARCC is a very conditional buy in a reduced position. I wouldn't build much more than a 0.5% position in the name if I were to initiate right now.

The truth is, I won't initiate at all, as I don't like the outlook.

Moody's has changed its outlook to negative, and we don't know what other blowups have not yet shown up across the sector.

An investor here would be forced to accept potential capital losses in the interim.

If you want exposure to high-yield BDCs throughout the cycle, then you would want to own ARCC.

I am less interested in permanent exposure and more in building when I have conviction, and at a sector level, I'm not confident at all.

This doesn't take anything away from ARCC as a company, but for me, for now, I will pass.

data.title: Fidus Investment Remains A Hold, But Is Getting Closer To A Buy
data.description:
I see many risks surrounding the BDC sector. Software vs. AI, the Fed's interest rates, etc. These are hot headlines that make Mr. Market very, very concerned about the whole sector. Yes, many BDCs are suffering with falling stock prices and higher cost of capital (as a result), but I like such situations.

Why do I like that? That's when Mr. Market creates some buying opportunities. Not all BDCs were created equal; I've been adding a lot to Ares Capital (ARCC), Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX), and Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL). I consider them top-quality in the sector, but there are some other names worth considering.

As most of you who read my articles from time to time probably know, I'm also invested in Fidus Investment (FDUS). The last time I covered it, I put it on hold. I was concerned with valuation given those risks mentioned above. Still, each article about FDUS when I rated it a buy shows double-digit total returns - you can review it here.

I will say it right from the get-go. I'm not upgrading FDUS to a buy. Not yet. I see some good news about the portfolio and FDUS' outlook on risks I mentioned earlier. But I would like to buy more at a lower price. I think there will be an opportunity to do that in 2026. I will take my chances on that, be patient, and hopefully average down my cost in FDUS.

Still, there's new data available that I'd like to discuss. Also, the management team shares some valuable insights regarding risk factors I mentioned above. That's why I decided to prepare this follow-up coverage.

Portfolio Update Shows FDUS' Resilience to AI Risk

I'm pasting the portfolio overview below. FDUS is somewhat diversified. Why somewhat? Geography is no issue here, but look at the industry. No wonder FDUS's stock price has declined during the last few months. The IT services sector has the largest share in FDUS' portfolio, so I think a comment from the management is in order here.

Here's how they view their exposure. I'm quoting from the Earnings Call I listened to:

Given the current environment, we wanted to address our software and tech-enabled services portfolio. Worth noting, we have been investing in software companies for over 10 years at Fidus alongside leading private equity firms, and it's been a strong performing industry vertical for us.

(...)

With regard to software-related businesses, this includes evaluating and ultimately getting comfortable not only with the company's growth prospects and market position, but importantly, each company's technology was risk, including AI risk over the past 3 years or so. At Q4 2025, our software and tech-enabled services portfolio, so our portfolio exposed to AI opportunities and risks was $464 million, which comprised of 92% first-lien debt, 4% junior debt and 4% equity. This portfolio is well diversified across 28 total names and all but one are backed by financial sponsors we know well who have significant expertise in the space, resulting in an average exposure per name of $17 million.

So, I see a large exposure here. $17m per target may not seem like a lot, but all of them still represent quite a large share. Still, I think the IT space is nuanced. Not all players were created equal, and some may be much more exposed to AI risk than the others. That's where FDUS' experience matters. Why is that? With a 10+ year track record in the sector, I expect the team to know how to cherry-pick. So far, I see that metrics confirm that:

The weighted average loan-to-value for this portfolio was 37%, well below our total portfolio weighted average loan-to-value of 44%. In addition, substantially all of our first-lien investments are highly structured investments with at least 2 maintenance covenants. In short, we feel extremely good about the health of this portfolio and its long-term outlook. In addition, the characteristics of our overall portfolio remain quite positive from a credit quality and capital preservation perspective.

I Invest in BDCs for Income. Is It Safe in the Case of FDUS?

I think it is. Yes, it may seem strange to some of you. Especially with some BDCs cutting their dividends (e.g., Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) or Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL)), FDUS is a different case.

I was very happy to see the chart above. FDUS had a regular DPS coverage of 115% in 2025, which is more than many of its peers show. On top of that, it had enough liquidity to pay supplementals, which I also enjoyed. I don't expect those supplementals to continue in the long term, but it was a nice cherry on top.

Last quarter, the DPS coverage by NII was 121%. It's more than enough to make me confident about FDUS's regular dividends. What I also like to see is that these payments are not dangerous to the capital, measured by NAV. The NAV increased from $19.33 to $19.55 in one year. Yes, it was flat since Q2 2025, but I read this as good news as well. Especially given generous supplemental payments. Look at the chart below. I think 2025 was a good year for FDUS, despite all the negative trends in the market.

Despite the Good Stuff, I Would Like to Buy FDUS at a Lower Price

How do I think about it? I see the BDC space as risky enough to present several opportunities in the next quarters. I think FDUS may turn out to be one of them. Imagine it trading at $17s per share - that's where I'd like to buy more.

Don't get me wrong, if some of you decide to buy now, I think it's a nice call. FDUS is very close to a buy in my view. In my case, it's just the fact that I'm already invested in it that makes me prefer to stay patient before involving more capital. Let's call it "risk management". I intend to increase my exposure to the BDC space in the next quarters, so I want to be very picky.

Do you want to buy now? I don't think you will regret it. Dividends are safe (regular ones), non-accruals are at a great level of ~1% at FV, and the management claims to have software exposure under control. How about the valuation? While it's higher than I'd like it to be, it's still reasonable. FDUS trades below 1x P/BV, which is a slight discount to NAV. That's not a bad place to start building position.

Still, out of personal preferences and portfolio-building strategy I described above, FDUS is a hold for me.
